description:
A data-engineering teammate has created a small, user-space “firewall manifest” that lives completely in your home directory.  
    The file is:  
        /home/user/etl_firewall_rules.conf  

    • It already exists and currently contains **exactly one line** (including the comment marker and initial space):  
        # ETL Pipeline Firewall Rules  

    For the PostgreSQL warehouse that powers the new ELT jobs, you must open the default PostgreSQL TCP port (5432).  
    The policy convention adopted by the team is:

        ALLOW <port>/<protocol> # <very short human note>

    1. Append a single new line that grants PostgreSQL access by following that convention.  
       The line to add must be spelled **exactly**:

        ALLOW 5432/tcp # Postgres access

       After the command succeeds, /home/user/etl_firewall_rules.conf must contain **two lines total**, in this precise order and with a trailing newline after each line:
            # ETL Pipeline Firewall Rules
            ALLOW 5432/tcp # Postgres access

    2. Immediately record what you just did in a small one-line log.  
       Create the file (it does not exist yet):

        /home/user/etl_firewall_apply.log

       The file must contain one line, verbatim (including the leading “Applied” word):

        Applied rule: ALLOW 5432/tcp # Postgres access

       Make sure the file ends with a single trailing newline character.

    NOTE FOR THE AGENT:
    • Only a **single terminal command** is strictly necessary to append the rule and write the log, but you may use intermediate commands if you find them clearer.  
    • You do NOT need root or sudo; everything happens in the user’s home directory.  
    • Be sure the paths and text match exactly—automated tests will fail on extra spaces, missing newline characters, or other deviations.
truth:
Initial state before the agent starts:
        File /home/user/etl_firewall_rules.conf already exists. Contents (1 line + newline):
            "# ETL Pipeline Firewall Rules\n"

    Expected final state after correct completion:
        1. /home/user/etl_firewall_rules.conf exists and contains exactly (including newlines):
            "# ETL Pipeline Firewall Rules\nALLOW 5432/tcp # Postgres access\n"

        2. /home/user/etl_firewall_apply.log exists and contains exactly (including newline):
            "Applied rule: ALLOW 5432/tcp # Postgres access\n"
